name: Trevélez and Cáñar Alpujarras Loop
name_original: Trevélez y Cáñar — vuelta por las Alpujarras
description: This difficult 53.7-mile road cycling route through the Alpujarras offers challenging climbs, rewarding descents, and panoramic views.
region_intro: Hiking around Torvizcón offers diverse terrain in the heart of La Alpujarra region, Granada. The area is characterized by the Sierra de la Contraviesa mountain range, providing views of the Sierra Nevada and contrasting landscapes with vineyards, fig, and almond trees. Trails often follow the depression of the River Guadalfeo and lead to natural features like gorges and waterfalls. This varied topography provides a range of hiking experiences from riverbanks to mountain slopes. Best hiking trails around Torvizcón The most popular hiking route is Medieval Path of La Alpujarra – circular between Busquístar, Pórtugos, Atalbéitar and Ferreirola (PR-A 299), a 4.4 miles (7.1 km) trail that takes about 2 hours 17 minutes to complete. This moderate route connects several historic Alpujarran towns. Another top favourite among local hikers is La Tahá Circular Trail, a moderate 3.1 miles (5.0 km) path. This trail offers scenic views of the surrounding valleys and traditional architecture. Local hikers also love the Pitres - Acequia Gorda - Pórtugos Trail, a 5.5 miles (8.9 km) trail leading through irrigation channels and natural waterfalls, often completed in about 3 hours 18 minutes. Hiking around Torvizcón is defined by the Sierra de la Contraviesa, the River Guadalfeo, and picturesque vineyards and almond groves. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy walks to more challenging ascents. The routes in Torvizcón are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 1500 reviews. More than 6000 hikers have used komoot to explore Torvizcón's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of landscapes can I expect to see while hiking around Torvizcón?
A: Hiking around Torvizcón offers a diverse range of landscapes. You'll traverse the rugged Sierra de la Contraviesa mountains, with expansive views of the majestic Sierra Nevada. The trails often follow the depression of the River Guadalfeo, leading through picturesque vineyards, fig, and almond groves, as well as deep valleys and canyons. You'll also encounter traditional whitewashed villages and terraced fields, characteristic of the Alpujarra region.

Q: Are there any easy hiking trails suitable for beginners or casual walkers in Torvizcón?
A: Yes, Torvizcón offers a good selection of easy trails. Out of over 160 routes in the area, approximately 50 are classified as easy, providing accessible options for those looking for a more relaxed walk. These trails often explore the charming local villages and gentler slopes.

Q: Can I find challenging hikes with significant elevation gain near Torvizcón?
A: Absolutely. For experienced hikers seeking a challenge, there are 23 difficult routes around Torvizcón. One notable option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e980521520/medieval-path-of-la-alpujarra-circular-between-busquistar-and-castaras-pr-a-299" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Medieval Path of La Alpujarra – circular between Busquístar and Cástaras (PR-A 299)&lt;/a&gt;, which covers nearly 16 km with over 860 meters of elevation gain, offering a strenuous but rewarding experience.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes available in the Torvizcón area?
A: Yes, many trails in the Torvizcón area are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. A popular moderate circular route is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e980683108/la-taha-circular-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Tahá Circular Trail&lt;/a&gt;, which is about 5 km long and offers scenic views of the surrounding valleys and traditional architecture. Another excellent option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e979978132/polopos-y-sorvilan-circular-de-los-almonds-por-la-contraviesa" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Polopos y Sorvilán – circular de los almonds por La Contraviesa&lt;/a&gt;, a moderate 10.7 km route.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I discover along the hiking trails?
A: The trails around Torvizcón lead to several impressive natural features and landmarks. You can explore deep gorges like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4399552" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Path through the Trevélez Gorge&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2214805" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tajo del Águila&lt;/a&gt;, or visit the stunning &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5288705" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tajo de Cortés Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;. There are also historical elements such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4399531" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Stone Bridge Over the Trevélez Gorge&lt;/a&gt; and the intriguing &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5538122" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Olías Caves&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any trails that pass by viewpoints or offer panoramic vistas?
A: Many trails in the region offer spectacular views, especially given Torvizcón's location in the Contraviesa mountains with the Sierra Nevada as a backdrop. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4975354" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto Molina Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt; is a notable spot for panoramic vistas. Routes through the Sierra de la Contraviesa often provide expansive views over the Guadalfeo river valley and the surrounding mountain ranges.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Torvizcón?
A: The Alpujarra region, including Torvizcón, is generally pleasant for hiking in spring (April-May) and autumn (September-October) when temperatures are milder. Summer can be hot, especially at lower elevations, but higher mountain trails offer cooler conditions. Winter hiking is possible, but some higher routes might be affected by snow, particularly in the nearby Sierra Nevada.

Q: Are there any family-friendly hiking options around Torvizcón?
A: While specific 'family-friendly' routes aren't explicitly detailed, the presence of 50 easy trails suggests many suitable options for families. These typically involve shorter distances and less elevation gain, allowing for a pleasant outing with children. Exploring the traditional whitewashed villages and their immediate surroundings can also be a delightful family activity.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the hiking trails in Torvizcón?
A: The Alpujarra region is generally welcoming to dogs on trails, but it's always advisable to keep them on a leash, especially in areas with livestock or protected wildlife. Always check local regulations for specific trails or national park areas like the Sierra Nevada, where rules might be stricter. Ensure your dog is well-behaved and that you carry waste bags.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy most about hiking in Torvizcón?
A: The hiking experiences around Torvizcón are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 1500 reviews. Hikers frequently praise the diverse terrain, from riverbanks to mountain slopes, the stunning views of the Sierra Nevada, and the charm of connecting traditional Alpujarran villages. The blend of natural beauty and cultural exploration is a consistent highlight.

Q: Are there any trails that feature traditional irrigation channels (acequias)?
A: Yes, the region is known for its historic irrigation channels. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e980711586/pitres-acequia-gorda-portugos-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pitres - Acequia Gorda - Pórtugos Trail&lt;/a&gt; is an excellent example, leading through these ancient acequias and past natural waterfalls, offering a unique insight into the traditional agricultural landscape of the Alpujarras.
tips: Orgiba, or Orgiva in English is the capital of the region and is a small town where you meet people living in alternative ways. On my last visit, I stayed at the campsite south of town. They are not that many and deserve to be pointed out. Orgiva is a good base camp to rest. The city center presents a monumental statue of the head of the hero of Cervantés: Don Quixote.
From the Cáñar turnoff it is 5km at an average of 7%
You have to be well trained to go and come back
Beautiful road that descends from the town of Pampaneira and Bubión that offers spectacular views of the Alpujarras and the set of small, steep and straight valleys.

It is famous for its hams, which are cured in the cold, dry air of the mountains. Trevélez is a beautiful town, with sloping streets and charming neighbors. It is a relevant tourist destination in the Alpujarras and serves as a base for many excursions through its spectacular surroundings.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete the Trevélez y Cáñar road cycling route?
faqs.answer: This challenging road cycling route is approximately 86.4 kilometers long with significant elevation gain. It typically takes around 5 hours and 16 minutes to complete, but this can vary based on your fitness level and how often you stop to enjoy the views.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route is primarily on well-paved roads, but it is classified as 'difficult' due to significant climbs. You'll encounter steep gradients, sometimes reaching up to 15%, and numerous switchbacks, especially on the ascents. Despite the challenge, the surfaces are generally smooth and easy to ride.

faqs.question: Is the Trevélez y Cáñar road cycling route suitable for beginners or families?
faqs.answer: No, this route is classified as 'difficult' and demands very good fitness from cyclists. With an elevation gain of approximately 2,300 meters and steep climbs, it is not recommended for beginners, families, or less experienced cyclists. It's best suited for advanced riders seeking a thrilling experience.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle the Trevélez y Cáñar route?
faqs.answer: The Alpujarra region generally offers good cycling conditions outside of the hottest summer months and coldest winter periods. Spring and autumn are often ideal, providing pleasant temperatures and stunning scenery. Always check local weather conditions before you go, as mountain weather can be unpredictable.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest or scenic views along the route?
faqs.answer: The route offers breathtaking views throughout the dramatic landscapes of the Alpujarras. You'll pass through charming white villages like Órgiva and the famous village of Trevélez, known for its Serrano ham. Key highlights include the challenging Climb to Cáñar, the Fountain of the Casa de Aguas, Cáñar, and numerous View of the Alpujarras Valleys, offering panoramic vistas as you gain elevation.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or resupply along the Trevélez y Cáñar route?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route passes through several villages where you can find amenities. Órgiva and Trevélez are notable settlements along the way that offer cafes, restaurants, and shops where you can refuel and resupply. It's always wise to carry enough water and snacks, especially for the longer stretches between villages.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the Trevélez y Cáñar road cycling route?
faqs.answer: While the starting point isn't explicitly defined, you can typically find parking in the larger villages along the route, such as Órgiva or Trevélez, which serve as good access points for this loop. Look for public parking areas within these towns.

faqs.question: Is the Trevélez y Cáñar road cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: This is a road cycling route, so while dogs are generally not suitable companions for cyclists on challenging road rides, there are no specific restrictions against them in the general area. If you plan to bring a dog, ensure they are safely secured in a bike trailer or carrier, and always adhere to local leash laws and clean-up regulations in villages and natural areas.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or fees required to cycle this route, especially within Sierra Nevada National Park?
faqs.answer: This route is located within the Sierra Nevada National Park. Generally, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required for road cycling on public roads within the park. However, always respect park regulations, stay on marked routes, and be mindful of wildlife and the natural environment. For specific activities or off-road cycling, permits might be necessary, but not for this road route.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other cycling trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route intersects with several other official trails for varying distances. Notably, it covers significant sections of the [CIMA GR14] Trevélez (46%) and Transnevada (Stage 6 / 8) (20%). Other intersecting trails include Transandalus (18%), [CIMA GR19] Portichuelo de Cástaras (16%), and Andalucía - South Route (Provincia de Granada) (16%).

faqs.question: Given the difficulty, are there any tips for tackling the climbs on this route?
faqs.answer: The climbs on this route are significant, with gradients up to 15% and numerous switchbacks. To tackle them effectively, ensure you have appropriate gearing on your racebike. Pacing yourself is crucial; start conservatively and maintain a steady rhythm. Utilize the quiet roads to focus on your effort and enjoy the stunning views as a reward for your hard work. Remember to stay hydrated and fueled throughout the ride.
